artifacts.content:
Dear Judy,

I’ve just made a pot of tea and finally have a quiet half hour, so I wanted to catch you up properly rather than in dribs and drabs over text.

The big thing is that I saw Carter & Lowe Solicitors in Bondi Junction on 14 March last year to update the will. It’s all very dull legally, but I feel calmer having it done. We’ve set up a discretionary testamentary trust because of Daniel’s situation – his gambling really took off around June 2021 and he’s now sitting on about $85,000 in personal loans and credit card debt between Westpac in the city and ANZ at Bondi Junction. The lawyer was quite firm that anything I want to leave for the children – especially Sophie, Olivia and little Lucas – needs to be protected so it can’t be swallowed up by his creditors or used to service those gambling debts.

At the same time we’ve tried to be realistic about Peter. After his stroke at Prince of Wales Hospital in Randwick back in October 2022, they gently raised the possibility that he may eventually need residential care. So the $120,000 I’ve earmarked for St Catherine’s may have to be reduced or postponed if he ends up at St Brigid’s Aged Care in Maroubra and we need to cover a refundable accommodation deposit and fees for three to five years.

All of that sounds very morbid written down, but day to day life is still full – choir on Thursdays, archives at school on Tuesdays, and walks when my knee behaves. How are things in Bowral? How is the garden coping with this heat?

With love,
M

metadata.subject: Proper update on wills and everything

artifacts.content:
Hi Em,

I’ve been meaning to put some of this in writing so you know how I’m thinking about things, especially with everything that’s happened over the last few years.

You remember how rocky 2020–21 was with my consulting – several of the Sydney independent schools simply froze their curriculum contracts during COVID, and my income dipped under $15,000 for that financial year. I was mortified to end up with $9,800 in unpaid BAS on my ABN and having to ring the ATO small business division to arrange a hardship plan. I don’t ever want to put you and Dan in a position where I die insolvent and you’re left cleaning up a mess, so the solicitor has put in a clause that the cash bequest to St Catherine’s can be reduced or even cancelled if my income falls away like that again.

At the same time, I’ve been thinking a lot about why I still want to leave something to the school at all. What Sophie went through in Term 2 2023 – the bullying and social media nastiness from that little group of Year 7 girls – broke my heart. I was so grateful for the three counselling sessions she had with the school psychologist in June 2023, and that long meeting I had with Mrs Helen Cartwright in her office on 22 June about mental health and digital citizenship. Part of what I’m leaving is specifically for that kind of pastoral care.

Anyway, enough estate talk. How are the girls going with homework this week? Do you need me for pickups on Wednesday as usual?

Love,
Mum

metadata.subject: A bit of context about my plans

artifacts.content:
Estate / family care notes – for my own reference

1. Daniel
- Gambling problem has been ongoing since around June 2021.
- Current unsecured debts approx. $85,000 (personal loans + credit cards) with Westpac (Sydney CBD branch) and ANZ (Bondi Junction).
- Testamentary discretionary trust in will (Carter & Lowe Solicitors, Bondi Junction – updated 14 March 2024) is intended to:
  • quarantine any inheritance earmarked for grandchildren (Sophie, Olivia, Lucas) from Daniel’s creditors;
  • ensure funds aren’t diverted to service his gambling debts.

2. Peter
- Stroke at Prince of Wales Hospital, Randwick – October 2022 (neurology team).
- If further stroke / decline and he needs residential care at St Brigid’s Aged Care, Maroubra:
  • bequest of $120,000 to St Catherine’s may need to be reduced or postponed;
  • priority = refundable accommodation deposit + 3–5 years of fees.

3. Judy
- Early-onset Alzheimer’s diagnosis at Bowral District Hospital – May 2023 (geriatrician).
- No children; if she requires full-time care / guardianship before I die:
  • up to $60,000 originally intended for St Catherine’s can be diverted to Bowral House Aged Care dementia unit.

4. My finances
- If consulting income drops below $15,000 p.a. again (like 2020–21 COVID year) and I end up back in hardship with ATO (e.g. the $9,800 unpaid BAS situation), executor may reduce/cancel St Cath’s cash bequest to avoid insolvency.

Remember: overall intent is to support St Catherine’s scholarships and pastoral care, but never at the expense of Peter’s care, Judy’s dignity, or basic solvency for the estate.

metadata.title: Estate and care priorities – working notes

artifacts.content:
Event: Will and estate review – focus on care contingencies & school bequest

Date: Friday, 2026-03-13
Time: 10:30 am – 12:00 pm
Location: Carter & Lowe Solicitors, Level 4, Bondi Junction
Attendees: Margaret Henderson; [solicitor]

Agenda / Notes:

1. Review discretionary testamentary trust set up in March 2024 to protect grandchildren’s inheritance from Daniel’s creditors.
   - Confirm wording re: Daniel James Henderson’s gambling-related debts (approx. $85,000 across Westpac Sydney CBD and ANZ Bondi Junction, originating around June 2021).
   - Ensure distributions for Sophie, Olivia and Lucas cannot be seized or used to service those debts.

2. Revisit conditional $120,000 bequest to St Catherine’s School Waverley.
   - Clarify triggers where funds may be reduced or postponed if Peter requires residential aged care.
   - Discuss practical costs if he moves into St Brigid’s Aged Care, Maroubra (RAD + 3–5 years of fees), following his October 2022 stroke at Prince of Wales Hospital, Randwick.

3. Confirm clause for Judith Anne Fielding.
   - Early-onset Alzheimer’s diagnosis at Bowral District Hospital, May 2023.
   - Up to $60,000 may be redirected from school bequest to fund placement at Bowral House Aged Care dementia unit if she needs full-time care before my death.

4. Check insolvency safeguard.
   - If consulting income falls under $15,000 p.a. again (as in 2020–21) and I face another ATO hardship arrangement like the $9,800 unpaid BAS, executor empowered to reduce/cancel school cash bequest.

Bring: latest super statements, rough budget, notes from last meeting with St Catherine’s about potential scholarship focus.

metadata.title: Will and estate review – Carter & Lowe

artifacts.content:
Hi Rob,

I’ve just come back from choir practice at church and thought I’d write while it’s all still fresh in my mind.

I’ve been thinking a lot about Judy since her early-onset Alzheimer’s diagnosis at Bowral District Hospital in May 2023. It still feels surreal that my “little” sister is facing this. The geriatrician was quite clear that we should be planning ahead for the possibility of full-time care. Because she has no children of her own, I’ve asked the solicitor to include a clause so that, if she needs to move into the dementia unit at Bowral House Aged Care before I go, up to $60,000 of what I’d originally intended to leave to St Catherine’s can be diverted to help cover the costs.

At the same time, I’ve tried to balance that with Peter’s situation after his stroke at Prince of Wales in Randwick in October 2022. If he deteriorates and we need to place him at St Brigid’s Aged Care in Maroubra, the $120,000 I’ve earmarked for the school may have to be trimmed or delayed to help with the refundable accommodation deposit and at least 3–5 years of fees.

I know this all sounds very clinical, but it actually makes me feel less anxious, knowing there’s a plan for both of them. Day to day, Peter is pottering in the garden and complaining about the Swans, and Judy still has flashes of her old wicked humour on the phone.

How are you managing work-wise? Are you still commuting into Lane Cove every day or doing more from home now?

Love,
Marg

metadata.subject: Judy, Peter and a bit of future planning

artifacts.content:
Dear Helen,

I hope the term has started smoothly and that you’ve had at least one quiet cup of coffee this week.

I wanted to follow up on our conversations last year about how I might support the school in a more concrete way. As you know, Sophie’s experience in Term 2 2023 – the bullying and social media harassment from that small group of Year 7 girls – was very hard on her and on Emily. I remain deeply grateful for the three counselling sessions she had with the school psychologist in June 2023, and for the time you gave me in your office on 22 June 2023 to talk through how St Catherine’s is approaching girls’ mental health and digital citizenship.

Those conversations, together with my own memories as an Old Girl and now a grandmother at the gates, have shaped my decision to leave a bequest to the school. My current will (done through Carter & Lowe in Bondi Junction) sets aside $120,000 for St Catherine’s, with a strong preference that it be used to support scholarships for regional girls and to strengthen pastoral care and digital citizenship programs.

There are, of course, some practical conditions: if Peter requires residential aged care after his stroke at Prince of Wales Hospital in Randwick in October 2022, or if my sister Judy’s early-onset Alzheimer’s (diagnosed at Bowral District Hospital in May 2023) progresses to the point where she needs the dementia unit at Bowral House, some of that amount may need to be redirected. But my clear intention, if circumstances allow, is that a substantial portion will come to the school.

If it would be helpful, I’m very happy to meet with you and someone from Community Relations to talk about how such support could be most useful.

Warm regards,
Margaret Henderson (’72)

metadata.subject: Bequest intentions and pastoral care focus

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – finances, work, and why the school still matters

Sitting on the back patio with a cup of tea, thinking about how precarious things felt in the 2020–2021 financial year. When COVID hit and several of the Sydney independent schools paused my consulting contracts, my income dropped below $15,000 and I honestly panicked. I can still remember dialling the ATO small business line, hands shaking, to talk to the case officer about the $9,800 in unpaid BAS on my ABN. The hardship arrangement they gave me was a lifeline, but I promised myself I’d never drift that close to the edge again.

That’s why the new will language matters: the executor can reduce or cancel the cash bequest to St Catherine’s if I’m back in that sort of trouble. It feels sensible rather than stingy.

And yet, I can’t quite imagine not leaving something to the school. Watching Sophie walk through those sandstone gates after everything that happened in Term 2 2023 – the nasty group chats, the anonymous posts – and then seeing how the school psychologist and Helen Cartwright rallied around her in June reminded me why I care. Three counselling sessions and one long meeting in Helen’s office on 22 June 2023 did more for her confidence than any amount of grandmotherly pep talks.

So: keep working while the knee holds up; aim to keep income safely above that $15k mark; and, if life cooperates, let the bequest stand as a quiet thank you to a place that shaped both me and Sophie.

Notes to self:

- Signed: Margaret L. Henderson
- Dated: 2026-01-22
